channel.title: My Blog
channel.description: A simple static blog built with Go

item.title: Building a Blog with Go
item.description: How I built this blog engine
item.content:encoded:
Building a Blog with Go

I built this blog engine from scratch using Go. Here's how it works:

Architecture

myblog/
├── cmd/           # CLI entry point
├── internal/       # Core packages
│   ├── config/    # Configuration
│   ├── content/   # Markdown parsing
│   ├── generator/ # HTML generation
│   ├── search/   # Search index
│   └── feed/     # RSS/Atom
├── themes/         # Theme templates
└── content/      # Blog posts

Key Packages

Markdown Parsing

Using goldmark for Markdown to HTML conversion with extensions for:

  • GFM (GitHub Flavored Markdown)
  • Syntax highlighting
  • Tables and task lists

Templating

Go's built-in html/template package provides safe templating with:

  • Layouts and partials
  • Custom functions
  • Automatic escaping

Frontmatter

YAML frontmatter in each post provides metadata:

  • Title, date, tags
  • Description
  • Draft status

Results

The blog generates static HTML that's fast, secure, and easy to host anywhere!

item.title: Static Site Generators
item.content:encoded:
Static Site Generators

Static site generators have become popular again. Here's why they're excellent choice:

Benefits

  1. Speed - Pre-rendered HTML loads instantly
  2. Security - No database or server-side code to hack
  3. Simplicity - Just files, easy to version control
  4. Scalability - CDN hosting, nearly unlimited traffic

Popular Options

  • Hugo - Very fast, written in Go
  • Jekyll - Ruby-based, GitHub Pages friendly
  • Eleventy - Node.js based, flexible
  • This blog - Custom Go implementation!

When to Use Static

  • Blogs and portfolios
  • Documentation sites
  • Landing pages
  • Any content that doesn't need real-time data

Static sites aren't right for every use case, but when they work, they work beautifully.

item.title: Markdown Features
item.content:encoded:
Markdown Features

Here are some of the Markdown features supported by this blog:

Lists

Unordered list:

  • Item one
  • Item two
  • Item three

Ordered list:

  1. First item
  2. Second item
  3. Third item

Code Blocks

Inline code and code blocks:

def hello():
    print("Hello from Python!")

function hello() {
    console.log("Hello from JavaScript!");
}

Blockquotes

This is a blockquote. It can be used for quotes or highlights.

Multiple lines work too!

Tables

Feature Support
Tables Yes
Code Yes
Links Yes

Links and Images

Visit Go website

Task Lists

  • Create blog engine
  • Add Markdown support
  • Write more posts
  • Deploy to production

item.title: Hello World
item.content:encoded:
Hello World

Welcome to my new blog! This is my first post written in Markdown.

Why Go?

I chose Go for this blog engine because:

  1. Fast compilation - Go compiles very quickly
  2. Simple syntax - Easy to read and maintain
  3. Great standard library - Lots of built-in functionality
  4. Static binaries - No runtime dependencies
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}

This is just the beginning. Stay tuned for more posts about Go, web development, and more!
